---
title: "Data science walkthrough 2"
format:
  revealjs:
    toc: false
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---




## From one region to many countries

Last time we looked at coverage and incidence in one specific region.

That works when the question is small enough to inspect directly.

This time, let's think about a bigger manuscript problem:

> How can we flag issues across multiple countries when there is too much data
> to look through manually?

## What changes today?

To make the repeated task concrete, we will use a simple flagging rule:

> Priority year = MCV1 coverage below 90% and at least 1 measles case per
> 100,000 people.

The workflow changes:

1. Write the rule once.
1. Test it on one country.
1. Repeat it across many countries.
1. Turn the repeated results into a table for the report.

## Load the data




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data_file <- here::here("data", "measles_final.Rds")

measles <- readr::read_rds(data_file)
```
:::




## Before we loop

Loops are easier when we know the exact task.

For one country, we need to:

1. Keep rows for that country and year range.
1. Calculate cases per 100,000 people.
1. Apply the priority-year rule.
1. Count the priority years.
1. Return one row of results.

. . .

Sounds like a good use of a function!

## Function 1: apply the rule




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
add_priority_rule <- function(country_data,
                              coverage_cutoff = 90,
                              incidence_cutoff = 1) {
  country_data$cases_per_100k <- (
    country_data$measles_cases / country_data$population * 100000
  )
  
  mcv_coverage_flag <- country_data$MCV1_coverage < coverage_cutoff
  
  incidence_flag <- country_data$cases_per_100k >= incidence_cutoff

  country_data$priority_year <- mcv_coverage_flag & incidence_flag

  return(country_data)
}
```
:::




## Test the rule on one country




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pakistan <- measles[
  measles$country == "Pakistan" & measles$year >= 2000,
]

pakistan <- pakistan[order(pakistan$year), ]
pakistan <- add_priority_rule(pakistan)

head(pakistan[, c("year", "MCV1_coverage", "cases_per_100k", "priority_year")])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 6 × 4
   year MCV1_coverage cases_per_100k priority_year
  <dbl>         <dbl>          <dbl> <lgl>        
1  2000            57           1.34 TRUE         
2  2001            59           2.42 TRUE         
3  2002            60           2.39 TRUE         
4  2003            62           2.84 TRUE         
5  2004            63           2.49 TRUE         
6  2005            61           1.71 TRUE         
```


:::
:::




## Function 2: summarize one country




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summarize_priority_years <- function(country_name,
                                     data,
                                     min_year = 2000,
                                     coverage_cutoff = 90,
                                     incidence_cutoff = 1) {
  country_data <- subset(
    data,
    country == country_name & year >= min_year
  )

  country_data <- add_priority_rule(
    country_data, coverage_cutoff, incidence_cutoff
  )

  priority_years <- subset(country_data, priority_year)$year

  data.frame(
    country = country_name,
    region = country_data$region[1],
    sub_region = country_data$sub_region[1],
    years = nrow(country_data),
    priority_years = length(priority_years),
    most_recent_priority_year = if (length(priority_years) == 0) {
      NA_integer_
    } else {
      max(priority_years)
    },
    max_cases_per_100k = if (all(is.na(country_data$cases_per_100k))) {
      NA_real_
    } else {
      round(max(country_data$cases_per_100k, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
    }
  )
}
```
:::




## Test the summary function




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summarize_priority_years("Pakistan", data = measles)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   country region    sub_region years priority_years most_recent_priority_year
1 Pakistan   Asia Southern Asia    24             18                      2022
  max_cases_per_100k
1              15.02
```


:::
:::




## Why this helps

Before writing a loop, we made one repeatable unit:

```r
summarize_priority_years("Pakistan", data = measles)
```

If that works for Pakistan, the loop only needs to change the country name.

## Repeat with a `for` loop




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
countries <- c("Afghanistan", "Bangladesh", "India", "Nepal", "Pakistan")

country_summaries <- data.frame()

for (country_name in countries) {
  one_summary <- summarize_priority_years(country_name, data = measles)
  country_summaries <- rbind(country_summaries, one_summary)
}
```
:::




## Repeat with a `for` loop: result




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
country_summaries
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      country region    sub_region years priority_years
1 Afghanistan   Asia Southern Asia    24             22
2  Bangladesh   Asia Southern Asia    24              9
3       India   Asia Southern Asia    24             15
4       Nepal   Asia Southern Asia    24             16
5    Pakistan   Asia Southern Asia    24             18
  most_recent_priority_year max_cases_per_100k
1                      2022              44.50
2                      2012              18.40
3                      2016               5.47
4                      2020              51.96
5                      2022              15.02
```


:::
:::




## What did the loop do?

Each loop pass did the same job:

1. Put one country name into `country_name`.
1. Run `summarize_priority_years()` for that country.
1. Add the one-row answer to the growing results table.

## Repeat with `lapply()`

The same idea can be written in a more functional style.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
summary_list <- lapply(
  countries,
  summarize_priority_years,
  data = measles
)

length(summary_list)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 5
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary_list[[1]]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      country region    sub_region years priority_years
1 Afghanistan   Asia Southern Asia    24             22
  most_recent_priority_year max_cases_per_100k
1                      2022               44.5
```


:::
:::




## Combine the list

- It's worth understanding how to use `do.call()` even though it's weird.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
country_summaries_lapply <- do.call(rbind, summary_list)

country_summaries_lapply
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
      country region    sub_region years priority_years
1 Afghanistan   Asia Southern Asia    24             22
2  Bangladesh   Asia Southern Asia    24              9
3       India   Asia Southern Asia    24             15
4       Nepal   Asia Southern Asia    24             16
5    Pakistan   Asia Southern Asia    24             18
  most_recent_priority_year max_cases_per_100k
1                      2022              44.50
2                      2012              18.40
3                      2016               5.47
4                      2020              51.96
5                      2022              15.02
```


:::
:::




## Which version should you use?

Use the version that helps you think clearly.

- A `for` loop is often easier to read while you are learning.
- `lapply()` is compact once the repeated task is already a function.
- Both are better than copying and pasting the same analysis five times.

## Now repeat over a whole region




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
asia <- measles[measles$region == "Asia", ]
asian_countries <- sort(unique(asia$country))

head(asian_countries)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Afghanistan" "Armenia"     "Azerbaijan"  "Bahrain"     "Bangladesh" 
[6] "Bhutan"     
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
length(asian_countries)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 50
```


:::
:::




## Run the country summary for Asia




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
asia_summary_list <- lapply(
  asian_countries,
  summarize_priority_years,
  data = asia
)

asia_summaries <- do.call(rbind, asia_summary_list)
```
:::




## Sort the results




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
asia_summaries <- asia_summaries[
  order(-asia_summaries$priority_years, asia_summaries$country),
]

head(asia_summaries)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       country region         sub_region years priority_years
1  Afghanistan   Asia      Southern Asia    24             22
14   Indonesia   Asia South-eastern Asia    24             19
34    Pakistan   Asia      Southern Asia    24             18
50       Yemen   Asia       Western Asia    24             17
32       Nepal   Asia      Southern Asia    24             16
13       India   Asia      Southern Asia    24             15
   most_recent_priority_year max_cases_per_100k
1                       2022              44.50
14                      2022              12.91
34                      2022              15.02
50                      2022             144.43
32                      2020              51.96
13                      2016               5.47
```


:::
:::




## Make a manuscript-style table




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_asia <- head(asia_summaries, 10)

top_asia_table <- top_asia[, c(
  "country",
  "sub_region",
  "priority_years",
  "most_recent_priority_year",
  "max_cases_per_100k"
)]

top_asia_counts <- top_asia_table[, c(
  "country",
  "sub_region",
  "priority_years"
)]

top_asia_details <- top_asia_table[, c(
  "country",
  "most_recent_priority_year",
  "max_cases_per_100k"
)]
```
:::




## Make a manuscript-style table: counts




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(top_asia_counts, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       country         sub_region priority_years
1  Afghanistan      Southern Asia             22
14   Indonesia South-eastern Asia             19
34    Pakistan      Southern Asia             18
50       Yemen       Western Asia             17
32       Nepal      Southern Asia             16
```


:::
:::




## Make a manuscript-style table: counts, continued




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
tail(top_asia_counts, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                            country         sub_region priority_years
13                            India      Southern Asia             15
25 Lao People's Democratic Republic South-eastern Asia             15
36                      Philippines South-eastern Asia             14
16                             Iraq       Western Asia             13
8                          Cambodia South-eastern Asia             12
```


:::
:::




## Make a manuscript-style table: details




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(top_asia_details, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       country most_recent_priority_year max_cases_per_100k
1  Afghanistan                      2022              44.50
14   Indonesia                      2022              12.91
34    Pakistan                      2022              15.02
50       Yemen                      2022             144.43
32       Nepal                      2020              51.96
```


:::
:::




## Make a quick figure




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
barplot(
  top_asia$priority_years,
  names.arg = top_asia$country,
  las = 2,
  ylab = "Priority years since 2000",
  main = "Countries in Asia with the most priority years"
)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Walkthrough2_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::




## You try it!

**Student prompt**

Repeat the regional analysis for Africa.

Your goal is to produce a table of the 10 countries in Africa with the most
priority years since 2000.

## You try it!: hint

Reuse the objects we already built.

You only need to change:

1. the region filter
1. the vector of country names
1. the object names, if you want them to be easier to read

## You try it!: solution




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
africa <- measles[measles$region == "Africa", ]
african_countries <- sort(unique(africa$country))

africa_summary_list <- lapply(
  african_countries,
  summarize_priority_years,
  data = africa
)

africa_summaries <- do.call(rbind, africa_summary_list)
```
:::




## You try it!: solution sort




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
africa_summaries <- africa_summaries[
  order(-africa_summaries$priority_years, africa_summaries$country),
]
```
:::




## You try it!: solution table




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
africa_top_columns <- africa_summaries[, c(
  "country",
  "priority_years"
)]

head(africa_top_columns, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                             country priority_years
8                           Cameroon             22
13 Congo, Democratic Republic of the             22
38                             Niger             22
39                           Nigeria             21
20                          Ethiopia             20
```


:::
:::




## You try it!: solution table, continued




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(africa_top_columns, 10)[6:10, ]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                    country priority_years
9  Central African Republic             19
3                     Benin             18
10                     Chad             18
12                    Congo             18
45                  Somalia             18
```


:::
:::




## You try it!: one more change

What if the manuscript team decides that 1 case per 100,000 is too sensitive?

Try the Africa analysis again with:

- MCV1 cutoff: 90%
- incidence cutoff: 5 cases per 100,000

## You try it!: solution




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
africa_summary_list_strict <- lapply(
  african_countries,
  summarize_priority_years,
  data = africa,
  coverage_cutoff = 90,
  incidence_cutoff = 5
)

africa_summaries_strict <- do.call(rbind, africa_summary_list_strict)
```
:::




## You try it!: strict solution sort




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
africa_summaries_strict <- africa_summaries_strict[
  order(-africa_summaries_strict$priority_years, africa_summaries_strict$country),
]
```
:::




## You try it!: strict solution table




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
africa_strict_top_columns <- africa_summaries_strict[, c(
  "country",
  "priority_years"
)]

head(africa_strict_top_columns, 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                             country priority_years
13 Congo, Democratic Republic of the             21
45                           Somalia             17
38                             Niger             16
39                           Nigeria             15
9           Central African Republic             13
```


:::
:::




## You try it!: strict solution table, continued




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
head(africa_strict_top_columns, 10)[6:10, ]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
    country priority_years
10     Chad             13
2    Angola             12
12    Congo             11
15 Djibouti             11
28  Liberia             11
```


:::
:::




## What did we practice?

- Turning one successful analysis into a function
- Repeating that function with a `for` loop
- Repeating that function with `lapply()`
- Combining repeated results with `do.call(rbind, ...)`
- Creating a manuscript-ready summary table from many repeated analyses

In Exercise 2, this same habit helps you move from a draft analysis to a
polished report.

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
